g.text: Cron-driven task dispatch: TOML sync on startup → tick loop → cron evaluation → dispatch mode routing → per-butler isolation
RUNTIME — TICK LOOP (single asyncio task per butler)
JOB REGISTRY — no LLM spawn, direct Python coroutine
until_at guard: if next_run_at > until_at → auto-disable task (enabled=false, next_run_at=NULL)
CancelledError: asyncio.shield(tick_task) — in-progress tick() finishes before loop exits on shutdown
CONCURRENCY MODEL — Scheduler Direct-Awaits dispatch_fn (No Queue)
No asyncio.Queue: tick() directly awaits dispatch_fn for each due task in serial order.
The Spawner's asyncio.Semaphore (not a queue) provides backpressure — scheduler blocks awaiting a free LLM session slot.
Deterministic jobs (registry path) skip the Spawner entirely: they run inline as Python coroutines.
PER-BUTLER ISOLATION — Each Butler Has Its Own Independent Scheduler
Each butler runs in its own OS process (separate daemon). No shared scheduler state. Inter-butler communication is MCP-only through Switchboard.
stagger_key = butler name → SHA-256 deterministic offset prevents thundering herd even when multiple butlers share the same cron boundary.
Each butler gets a deterministic offset: SHA-256(butler_name) mod min(15min, cron_cadence-1) → spread DB load across time, no thundering herd on shared cron boundaries
TICK LOOP — 6-STEP NUMBERED SEQUENCE (per tick() call)
OpenTelemetry: butler.tick span wraps all 6 steps — attributes: tasks_due (count queried), tasks_run (count dispatched)
BUFFER INTEGRATION — Scheduler vs. Message Buffer (Switchboard Ingress)
Key distinction: Scheduler fires tasks on a cron schedule (time-driven), writing trigger_source="schedule:<name>" to the Spawner.
Message Buffer handles inbound messages (event-driven), routing via priority tiers to pipeline.process() → Spawner.
Both paths share the same Spawner and its asyncio.Semaphore backpressure — concurrent slot budget is shared.
calendar and send a prep summary.
